questions.prompt: Why use AWS Secrets Manager for database passwords instead of putting them in environment variables at deploy time?

choices.text: Rotation, audit, and reduced exposure. Secrets in env vars sit there indefinitely — same value until someone manually changes it, visible to anyone who can describe the running container or inspect the deployment configuration, included in any backup or snapshot of the deployment. Secrets Manager keeps secrets encrypted at rest via KMS, accessed via IAM-controlled API calls (every read is logged in CloudTrail), and supports automatic rotation for RDS/Aurora/DocumentDB (updates the database password and the secret atomically on a schedule). The application code only references the secret name; the actual value is retrieved at runtime. If a secret is leaked or compromised, rotation is a click; if env-var secrets leak, rotation requires redeploying every running instance. Cost is small ($0.40/secret/month) compared to the operational benefit. For database passwords, API keys, OAuth secrets, and similar credentials, Secrets Manager is the right pattern; Parameter Store SecureString is acceptable for lighter-weight secrets that don't need rotation.

questions.explanation: Secrets Manager is operational infrastructure for secrets. Env-var secrets are leftover from a pre-cloud era. Modern apps fetch from Secrets Manager at runtime; rotation becomes a non-event.

questions.prompt: An auditor asks for evidence that all EBS volumes in production are encrypted. What's the most efficient way to demonstrate this?

choices.text: AWS Config + Security Hub. AWS Config has a built-in rule called `encrypted-volumes` that continuously evaluates whether all EBS volumes meet the encryption requirement and reports compliance status. Security Hub aggregates Config findings (and many other sources) into a unified dashboard with compliance scores by control framework. Together they produce continuous, auditable evidence: which volumes are compliant, when each was created, when (if ever) any failed compliance. Beyond the immediate audit, set EBS encryption to default-on at the account level so newly-created volumes can't be unencrypted by mistake (`EC2 → Settings → EBS encryption → Enable by default`). For ongoing compliance, define a Conformance Pack with rules for all your relevant encryption requirements (EBS, S3, RDS, etc.) — Config evaluates them continuously and reports drift. Manual checks don't scale and aren't auditable evidence; Config + Security Hub provide the continuous compliance posture auditors expect.

questions.explanation: Continuous compliance via Config and Security Hub is the modern audit pattern. Define rules once, get evidence continuously, automate remediation where possible. Beats one-time manual audits in every dimension.
